# Doreen Mayrell (College Algebra)

<!-- vimeo: 1111475474 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Hello, my name's Doreen Mayra. I'm a math professor at Collin College in Plano, Texas. I teach everything from developmental math to calculus, which means I've heard the question, when am I ever going to use this a thousand times? Instead of giving the same old answer, I decided to rethink the entire model. I built a flipped classroom model where the AI tutor takes over the lecture. Students work through guided notes at home for homework, and they're structured with direct instruction principles to work on at their own pace. Some students finish their homework fairly quickly, getting through the lecture of the AI tutor, and some take longer, but they're being taken back to all the prerequisites and making sure that they really have a solid foundation filling in any gaps that they might have. Along the way, there's no guessing, no YouTube rabbit holes just clean, clear explanations from their new AI study buddy. Then they come to class, and here's where it gets wild. We use paper and pencil and calculators, no screens, no ai. We're just working those problems by hand. It's practically vintage. To deliver the AI guided instruction, I built custom GPT for each lesson. These are trained on the exact materials, so textbook sections, lesson objectives, and my own teaching approaches. It guides students through concepts using clear direct instruction, adjusting based on their responses. Honestly, it just mirrors what good teaching should look like, structured, supportive, and it's never tempted to say, didn't I just explain this? The process is anchored in guided notes and it follows the I do we do. You do Model one problem fully worked, one broken up into steps, and then one for independent practice. These notes don't just help the student. They train the AI on how to teach the topic effectively. So by the time the students arrive in class, they've already had a solid foundation, and I don't need to lecture them. That means we skip the easy stuff and we spend our time on the complex problems, the ones that are worth solving together. So in class, instead of lecture, we start off with pure skills practice and we switch to real world applications. So we might do something like use Exponential decay to Model battery drain from screen time and background apps, or compare the cost of daily coffee runs versus investing in a decent coffee Machine Or predicting social media follower growth from engagement metrics and even outside these projects, our day-to-day work is full of custom GPT use from personalizing practice problems to generating instant feedback. It's just blended naturally into the learning process. One of my favorite uses for custom gpt is taking a set of math problems and re-imagining them in the context that matters to the student. So sometimes that's career exploration or sometimes it's just something that they're interested in. Um, a sport, a TV show, and what happens is the AI will take a set of problems that I give it from a boring textbook set, and based on the student's interests, it'll transform so that each question is now into the context that really interests and engage the student. Because math doesn't just live in textbook, it's already in their world. So my job is to help them see it. Here's an example of a boring problem about marbles. Approximately no one will relate to this problem. I don't know about you, but I've never had to sort marbles into two piles in my entire adult life. It's a system of equations problem. We're finding out how many marbles are in each problem. It motivates no one. But what if we were to reinvent it? For students whose passion is fashion now instead of marbles, it's luxury, leather, handbags and canvas, handbags, same math, same steps, but now she connects it on multiple levels. Suddenly it's not just math, it's her world. Or take the same problem and hand it to a gamer. Instead of piles of marbles, they're calculating how many legendary items and epic items they've collected in their favorite game. Their prices are in gold coins instead of dollars. The math hasn't changed. It's still the same math of systems of equations, but now they're more invested because they're solving for something they actually care about. It feels less like homework and more like leveling up. And for the Taylor Swift fan, those two piles become floor tickets and balcony tickets for her next concert. Again, it's the same equation, same process, same solution, but the students hooked because it's about something they'd actually talk about outside of class. Here's the most important part, the math is exactly the same. The context changes with the equation, the steps and the solution path, they're identical. That's what makes this approach so powerful. It keeps the rigor, but lets every student see themselves in the problem. Another way I use GBT through custom assistance, where students can submit a practice test or a study guide and get immediate feedback. It's not just right or wrong, it tells them exactly where their thinking went off track and how to fix it. It's like having office hours 24 7 if office hours didn't require scheduling or pants, even better. It creates a personalized study guide with new practice questions tailored to that student's specific misunderstandings. It's adaptive, effective and way more helpful than staring at a red X wondering what went wrong. The result, students come to class with questions instead of blank stares. They get instant feedback without waiting for me to grade 140 papers by hand, and they care more because the math finally feels like it belongs in their world. Meanwhile, I get to spend class time doing what professors actually enjoy, discussing ideas, fixing misconceptions, and not repeating the foil method for the 17th time that morning, and the data backs it up. In the class where I used this model, the pass rate was 96%, and the average course grade was a 92. Both the average and the median final exam were 86. In a college algebra course that's nationally known for low pass rates nationally, more than a million students enroll in college algebra each year, and yet 40 to 50% of them do not manage to complete the course successfully. So that's how I use AI to redesign my classroom. It's not about replacing teachers, it's about offloading the routine tasks so we can focus on deeper thinking, real conversations, and the kind of problem solving that actually sticks. And if you've ever thought there has to be a better way to teach this, there is. It's powered by ai, fueled by imagination and requires letting go of what's comfortable to make room for what's transformational.
